exemplar.title: An investigation into the effect of the dual-processing model on cognitive abilities.

detailedExemplarData.title: An investigation into the effect of the dual-processing model on cognitive abilities.

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and precise statement of the investigation aim.
Relevance to study strategies and peer learning is well explained.
Theory of System 1 and System 2 is accurately described and linked to font fluency.
Variables are operationalised in the hypothesis with defined IV and DV.
weaknesses: Limited illustrative example of heuristic defaults to deepen the theoretical link.
Stylistic awkwardness in hypothesis wording reduces clarity slightly.
Connection between heuristic mitigation and font disfluency could be more explicit.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three introduction strands fall solidly in the 3â4 band but just short of the top. The aim is clear and relevant, theory/model is wellâtied to the investigation, and variables are operationalised, yet minor depth and stylistic issues prevent a full 5â6 band score. A holistic mark of 4/6 reflects strong performance with scope for deeper illustrative detail.

strengths: Research design is not only described but fully explained with rationale for independent measures.
Sampling technique and participant characteristics are detailed and justified.
Materials and controlled variables are identified with initial rationale.
weaknesses: Explanation of how each control mitigates specific confounds is uneven.
No reflection on the limitations of convenience sampling or its impact on generalisability.
Brief rationale for material choice lacks full depth on potential biases.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The design and sampling strands are in the top band, but controlled variables and materials only just move beyond description. The inconsistent depth of explanations places this criterion in the mid band, meriting 3/4.

strengths: Appropriate use of descriptive statistics and justification of the MannâWhitney U test.
Clear graphical presentation addressing the hypothesis with correctly labelled axes.
Interpretation connects findings to hypothesis and theoretical framework.
weaknesses: Criticalâvalue comparison contains an error in sample size reference.
No effectâsize measure is reported to supplement pâvalues.
Brief acknowledgement of test assumptions is missing.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Descriptive and inferential methods are correctly chosen and linked to the hypothesis, and the graph is well presented. However, minor statistical miscues and omission of effectâsize indicators prevent full topâband achievement, justifying a 4/6.

strengths: Findings are discussed in relation to Kahnemanâs dualâprocessing model and Alter et al.
Strengths and limitations are explained with direct relevance to validity and generalisability.
Modifications are explicitly linked to limitations and fully justified.
weaknesses: Discussion could acknowledge alternative explanations (e.g., novelty effects).
Claim of high ecological validity conflates convenience sampling with realism without nuance.
Educational implications would benefit from addressing potential cognitive fatigue.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation is strong in linking results to theory, detailing relevant strengths/limitations, and proposing wellâjustified modifications. Minor lapses in nuance regarding alternative explanations and ecological validity keep this criterion at 5/6 rather than full marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart with error bars is supplied, clearly labelled and directly addresses the hypothesis by contrasting mean CRT scores across conditions.  Axis titles and condition labels are present, though the y-axis starts at zero and spelling is sound; slight aesthetic limitations justify 5 rather than 6 but keep it in the top band.

Findings are **interpreted in light of descriptive and inferential outputs** and explicitly linked back to acceptance/rejection of hypotheses and to System 1/2 engagement.  The interpretation is sound, though brief acknowledgement of effect size is missing, warranting a strong 5 within the top band.

The hypothesis section names the IV (font fluency, operationalised by size/colour/italics) and the DV (number of correct CRT answers, score 0â3). Both are **clearly operationalised**, and null/research hypotheses are stated in directional form.  Minor wording awkwardness does not reduce clarity, supporting a mark of 5 in the top band.

Convenience sampling is described and then **justified in terms of accessibility, ethical ease, and relevance to the research focus**.  Participant characteristics (age, education level, language skills) are detailed and linked to the investigationâs goals, fulfilling the âexplainedâ requirement.  Hence the strand earns 4/4 within the 3-4 band.

The dual-processing model is **accurately described**, distinguishing System 1 and System 2 with supporting literature, and cognitive miserliness and heuristics are elaborated.  The student then **explicitly ties this theoretical framework to the present manipulation of font fluency** and System 2 activation, demonstrating a clear explanatory link.  Depth is very good, though not exceptional enough to merit the absolute top of the band; thus 5/6 is appropriate.

The student goes beyond description, **discussing findings in the context of Kahneman and Alter et al.** as well as cognitive miserliness and educational implications.  The discussion is coherent and analytical, although not exceedingly nuanced; therefore 5/6 is justified.

The student states a clear aim â to examine whether disfluent font increases System 2 activation in 72 high-school students â and then **explains why this matters for personal study strategies and peer learning**.  The relevance is not merely identified but is explicitly justified in terms of potential cognitive-skill enhancement for the target population, satisfying the highest descriptor.  Minor stylistic issues do not detract from the fully articulated explanation, so a strong 5 within the 5-6 band is warranted.

Multiple strengths (control of extraneous variables, standardized messages) and limitations (convenience sample, prior CRT exposure, gender bias) are **explained in relation to validity and generalisability**, matching the top-band descriptor.  Depth is strong but not exhaustive, placing it at 5.

Controlled variables (content, visibility, timing, seating, messages) and materials (two CRT formats) are identified with brief rationales, but the explanation of **why each control mitigates specific confounds** is uneven.  The work clearly surpasses simple description yet lacks the consistent depth shown elsewhere, so a mid-band 3 is most fitting.

The student offers several concrete, **explicitly linked and fully justified modifications** (e.g., screen for CRT familiarity, use alternate CRT versions, include multiple schools, pre-test math/English skills, gender/dyscalculia conditions).  Each stems directly from a stated limitation and includes a clear rationale for improvement, satisfying the highest expectations; hence full 6 marks.

The student not only describes but **explains** why a true experiment with independent measures was chosen, linking it to control of extraneous variables and avoidance of order effects.  This meets the criteria for the 3-4 band and shows solid elaboration, meriting the top of that band (4).

Means, SDs, a Mann-Whitney U were applied.  The choice of non-parametric test is justified (non-normal data) and a p-value reported.  However, the critical-value comparison is partially incorrect and raw-data table contains miscoded entries, indicating **minor errors** despite generally appropriate analysis.  This aligns with the 3-4 band; a 4 reflects mostly accurate application with small flaws.

annotations.comment: The student provides a clear and accurate description of System 1 and System 2 processing, demonstrating solid understanding of the dual-processing model.
The explanation of cognitive miserliness is solid, but an illustrative example of when individuals default to System 1 would deepen comprehension and strengthen the link to your hypothesis.
Heuristic use is defined generally here but lacks explicit connection to how font disfluency mitigates heuristic shortcuts in your own study.
The summary of Alter et al. (2007) is comprehensive and directly tied to your manipulation, effectively grounding your replication in prior research.
You note changes to Alter et al.âs design but should explicitly justify why individual seating and font choice enhance internal validity or applicability to high school students.
The aim is clearly stated and its relevance to improving peer learning and study strategies is effectively justified.
The IV is operationalised precisely by font characteristics, ensuring clarity in what is manipulated during the experiment.
Graph 1 clearly presents group differences, but adding effect-size indicators (e.g., r or Cohenâs d) would enhance interpretation.
Dependent variable is defined clearly in terms of CRT score, neatly tying System 2 activation to measurable outcomes.
The convenient sampling method is described, but you should discuss its limitations on generalisability and potential biases arising from accessibility.
Demographic details (age, track, proficiency) are well-specified, linking sample characteristics to the studyâs educational context.
Controlled variables are listed, but you need to explain how each control specifically mitigates confounding variables in this context.
Ethical procedures are thorough, with standardized messages and consent, reflecting strong consideration of participant welfare.
Table of means and SDs is well-formatted and directly supports comparison between conditions.
The discussion of pre-testing English and math skills addresses individual differences and improves reliability for future replications.
The rationale for choosing mean and SD is solid; consider briefly discussing why median wouldnât suffice given potential outliers.
Selection of the Mann-Whitney U test is justified by non-normality, demonstrating appropriate inferential choice.
The critical-value comparison using nâ = 20 is incorrect; you should consult the proper critical values for nâ = nâ = 36 or use z-approximation.
Including an effect-size measure and comment on test assumptions (ties, distribution) would strengthen the inferential analysis.
Discussion links findings back to Kahneman and Alter et al., demonstrating effective integration of data and theory.
The educational implications are compelling, but consider addressing whether disfluency might also induce cognitive fatigue.
The argument for cause-and-effect is strong, yet you could briefly acknowledge alternative explanations (e.g., novelty effect of grey font).
Strengths in internal validity are well argued, highlighting control of extraneous variables and standardized procedures.
The claim of high ecological validity conflates convenience sampling with ecological realism; consider refining this evaluation.
You acknowledge sample limitations; to strengthen this, explicitly link proposed multi-site sampling to enhancing population validity.
Gender bias is noted; you could enhance this by proposing analytic plans for separate gender or dyscalculia subgroups.
Some inconsistencies in reference formatting (italics, punctuation) appear; ensure consistent style according to APA or chosen format.
